exemplar.title: How do different sports (volleyball, taekwondo, swimming) and sex (male, female) influence visual reaction time [ms] using the color-changing application âReaction Speedâ?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do different sports (volleyball, taekwondo, swimming) and sex (male, female) influence visual reaction time [ms] using the color-changing application âReaction Speedâ?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is focused and embedded in a wellâdefined, specific context (adolescent athletes in three sports, two sexes).
Methodology description is detailed (apparatus, steps, sample size, cleaning, data recording) allowing reproducibility.
Methodological considerations are clearly explained, with biological rationale for each control.
weaknesses: Minor language and typographical slips (e.g. â¹Ïââââº symbol, ANOVA spelling) introduce small ambiguities.
No randomization of trial order or interâtrial intervals to control order effects.
Ethical section omits details on data confidentiality and participant privacy handling.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly in the 5â6 band, each earning a 5. The research question is described in a specific context, the methodology is reproducible, and controls are wellâexplained. Minor language errors and missing randomization details prevent a topâband (6) award but justify a 5.

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated with clarity and precision (labeled tables, graphs with units and error bars).
Uncertainties are appropriately considered (device resolution, SD, SEM, ANOVA outputs).
weaknesses: A calculation error in the mean (division by 2 instead of 5) constitutes a significant omission.
No postâhoc test is applied following a significant ANOVA on sport groups.
Uncertainty propagation is asserted but not fully shown; table formatting could be improved.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands demonstrate 5â6 band performance, but the processing strand has significant omissions and inaccuracies (mean formula error, missing postâhoc). A holistic reduction to 4 reflects strong overall analysis yet acknowledges these notable gaps.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question, is fully consistent with analysis, and cites mean values and Pâvalues as justification.
weaknesses: Comparison to scientific context is brief and superficial; does not deeply integrate or critique external studies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The relevance and consistency strand sits in the top band (5â6), but the scientific comparison is only at the 3â4 band. A holistic score of 3 reflects a wellâjustified conclusion undermined by cursory literature integration.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses and limitations are identified and linked to the study design.
Realistic and relevant improvements are described for each identified weakness.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness is not quantified or prioritized.
Discussion of limitations lacks depth on how they influenced the results quantitatively.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands fall in the 3â4 band: weaknesses are specific and improvements are detailed, but the report stops short of explaining the relative impact of limitations. A score of 3 reflects this solid but nonâexplanatory evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion directly answers the research question, cites mean values and ANOVA P-values as evidence, and maintains consistency with presented data (taekwondo fastest, males quicker). No contradictory statements appear. This aligns with a âjustified, fully consistentâ conclusion; minor numerical rounding slips warrant 5 rather than 6.

Raw tables are extensive and labelled; processed tables, sample calculations, and two graphs are presented with titles and units. Uncertainties accompany values and error bars are included. Layout is generally neat, though some table headings wrap awkwardly. Both clarity and precision are demonstrated, meriting the 5â6 band; minor formatting issues justify a 5 rather than 6.

Means, SDs, SEMs and two one-way ANOVAs are executed, which are suitable for the questions posed. However, an example mean calculation shows division by 2 instead of 5, and no post-hoc test is applied after a significant three-group ANOVA. These inaccuracies constitute âsignificant omissionsâ, placing the strand in the 3â4 band; the upper mark (4) is awarded because the bulk of processing is still appropriate and largely accurate.

Three concrete weaknesses are identified (age range, sample size, learning effect). Each is specific to the methodology and its potential influence is explained qualitatively, satisfying the descriptor for âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ. The impact is not fully quantified, so the top band is not reached; a 4 within the 3â4 band is appropriate.

The student briefly compares findings with two external studies (sex differences confirmed; lack of comparable branch study noted). While relevant, the discussion is cursory and does not deeply integrate or critique literature. This reflects âsome relevant comparisonâ fitting the 3â4 band; limited depth supports a score of 3.

The student formulates a focused research question and embeds it in a clearly defined context: adolescent athletes (16â18 yrs) in three contrasting sports and two sexes. Background literature is cited, the biological rationale for expecting differences is articulated, and the sampling constraints of the local sports school are explained. This specificity goes beyond a broad outline and matches the descriptor for a âspecific and appropriate contextâ, warranting placement in the 5â6 band. Minor language slips are present but do not hinder contextual clarity, so the lower end (5) of the band is appropriate.

Device resolution (Â±0.5 ms), standard deviation, standard error, and ANOVA outputs are all reported. Uncertainty reasoning (half least count) is valid and consistently applied. While propagation of Â±0.5 ms to means is asserted rather than fully shown, the consideration is appropriate and free of major omissions, fitting the top band. Small propagation detail gaps keep the score at 5.

Realistic improvements are proposed for every listed weakness (age stratification, recruiting more athletes, alternative reaction-time apparatus). Each suggestion matches its corresponding flaw and is described with sufficient detail to be actionable, meeting the descriptor for âdescribedâ improvements. The relative impact is not thoroughly prioritised, so the strand sits at the upper end of the 3â4 band with a score of 4.

Multiple methodological considerations are identified and **explained**: participant health, caffeine/alcohol, device latency, room lighting, distance from screen, time-of-day, dominant hand, temperature, equal group sizes, etc. Each control is linked to a clear biological reason for influence on reaction time, demonstrating understanding of why they matter and how they support sufficiency of data. While the discussion is thorough, some uncontrolled factors (intelligence, personality) are only stated, not mitigated, so awarding the lower mark (5) within the top band is justified.

The apparatus list, preparation steps and eight-step procedure allow another investigator to reproduce the study. Distances are quantified, number of trials fixed, device and application named, cleaning protocols noted, and raw-data recording method shown. A few typographical slips (e.g. division by 2 in example calculation) introduce minor ambiguity but do not materially hinder replication, placing the work solidly in the top band; the lower mark (5) reflects those small issues.

annotations.comment: The independent variables (sport and sex) are explicitly stated and clearly defined, facilitating reproducibility and clarity in the study design.

The introduction is verbose and could better focus on the specific research context. The student should refine the opening paragraph to emphasize the biological rationale for studying visual reaction time differences among sport disciplines.
Background citations describe sex and activity influences, but the student should explicitly link each source to the expected differences in reaction time for this investigation.
While the sample size of 10 per subgroup is stated, the student should justify this choice with reference to statistical power or precedent to strengthen the methodological rationale.
The research question is concise, well-defined and directly addresses the variables of interest (sport discipline and sex), providing a clear focus for the investigation.
The student lists caffeine, learning and fatigue controls with rationale, demonstrating thorough attention to external factors that could influence reaction times.
Uncontrolled variables are acknowledged, but intelligence and personality warrant deeper discussion. The student should propose ways to measure or mitigate their potential influence.
Table 1 comprehensively presents controlled variables, methods and reasons, demonstrating clear methodological planning for consistent experimental conditions.
The control list begins with an inexplicable math symbol â¹Ïââââº. The student should correct or remove this notation to avoid confusion in the controlled-variables section.
The raw data table is extensive and includes uncertainty, but its density hampers readability. Reorganizing into subâtables or summary statistics may improve clarity.
The mean calculation incorrectly divides by 2 instead of 5. The student must correct the formula to \(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^5 t_i}{5}\) for accurate data processing.
Uncertainty propagation is asserted but not shown. The student should detail how the Â±0.5 ms instrument error was carried through mean and SEM calculations.
To minimize order effects, the student should include randomization of trial sequence or rest intervals between trials in the methodology description.
The apparatus list is detailed and complete, enabling replication of the experiment with all necessary equipment clearly specified.
No mention is made of inter-trial intervals or random trial order. The student should introduce breaks or randomization to reduce learning and fatigue biases.
The ethical section omits data confidentiality and participant privacy. The student should detail how personal data were stored and anonymized.
Qualitative observations are noted (e.g. gaming habits), but the method for recording and integrating them into the analysis is unclear. The student should specify how such data were captured.
The table of qualitative data lacks clear categories or counts. The student should organize qualitative findings systematically to support later interpretation.
Including Â±0.5 ms uncertainties in Table 3 adds precision and demonstrates appropriate consideration of measurement limits.
Processed data headings in Table 4 could be clearer by repeating units [msâÂ±â0.5] and specifying sample size (n) directly in the column labels.
Summary Table 5 successfully presents sportâgroup means, SD and SEM, enhancing clarity in the comparison of group statistics.
No post-hoc test is applied following significant ANOVA on sport branches. The student should perform a Tukey or similar to identify which groups differ.
Graph 1 effectively visualizes group differences, but annotating p-values or significance bars would strengthen the presentation of statistical results.
Graph 2 clearly shows the sex difference, but the student should label error bars as SEM and include numeric values on the axes for precision.
The conclusion effectively restates findings but contains a typographical error ('ANNOVA'). The student should correct to 'ANOVA' and ensure consistency in terminology.
Comparison with literature is brief. The student should integrate external studies more deeply into the conclusion to contextualize results and highlight novel contributions.
The evaluation section lists general human error but lacks specific methodological weaknesses. The student should detail which design limitations most influenced the results.
Weaknesses table links each limitation to an improvement, demonstrating relevant evaluation of specific methodological issues.
The proposed CRT improvement is relevant and detailed, offering a realistic alternative method for future studies to enhance measurement complexity.

strands.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: A conclusion is described that is relevant to the research question but is not fully consistent with the analysis presented.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: A conclusion is justified that is relevant to the research question and fully consistent with the analysis presented.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison
strands.content: A conclusion is justified through relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.

strands.name: Improvements

strands.name: Improvements
strands.content: Realistic improvements to the investigation, that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are explained.
